one_line_takeaway: A rigorous first census of the agent-skills supply chain: SkillScan flags 26.1% of 31,132 real skills as containing dangerous patterns, but the authors show only ~5.2% look genuinely malicious and script-bundling skills carry 2.12x the risk—arguing for capability-based permissions and mandatory pre-publication scanning.
key_finding: Of 31,132 skills, 26.1% (8,126) were flagged with at least one dangerous pattern (adjusted/uncertainty range 23-30%). But this headline number is deliberately deconstructed: only 5.2% (1,619) show high-severity 'likely malicious' patterns, 8.1% (2,522) medium/ambiguous, and 12.8% (3,985) only low-severity 'likely negligent' patterns (e.g., unpinned deps, excessive permissions). By category, data exfiltration (13.3%, 4,133 skills) and privilege escalation (11.8%, 3,671) dominate, while prompt injection is rare (0.7%, 209). Skills bundling executable scripts are 2.12x more likely to be vulnerable (40.6% vs 24.2%, OR=2.12, p<0.001) than instruction-only skills; skill size >500 lines gives OR=2.14. Security/Red-team skills have the highest raw flag rate (67.4%) but this is 'dangerous by design' conflation—adjusted to ~21.4% after manual review. Recent maintenance shows NO significant protective effect (OR=0.91, p=0.47).
core_contribution: The first large-scale empirical security measurement of the 'agent skills' ecosystem (modular SKILL.md + bundled-script packages that extend AI coding agents such as Claude Code, Codex CLI, and Gemini CLI). The authors crawled 42,447 skills from two marketplaces (skills.rest, skillsmp.com), filtered to 31,132 unique skills, and built SkillScan, a three-stage detector (rule-based static scanner + LLM-Guard input scanners + Claude 3.5 Sonnet hybrid classifier) to flag vulnerabilities. The paper's own deliverables are: (1) a 14-pattern / 4-category vulnerability taxonomy grounded in open coding of a 500-skill development set; (2) the SkillScan detection framework achieving 86.7% precision and 82.5% recall against a 200-skill manually annotated ground truth; and (3) a released dataset of 31,132 labeled skills plus the collection and detection tooling. Headline empirical finding: 26.1% of skills contain at least one dangerous pattern.
threat_model: Adversaries publish skills to marketplaces lacking mandatory security review; users adopt them and the agent executes bundled code with the user's own permissions. Three adversary classes: (1) Malicious Authors (intentional data exfiltration / persistence / behavior manipulation, including natural-language instructions that bypass code-level scanning); (2) Supply Chain Attackers (previously benign skills compromised via account takeover, dependency confusion, or repo hijacking); (3) Negligent Developers (unintentional insecure code, excessive permissions). The common enabler is the 'consent gap'—the mismatch between what a user approves once and what the skill can subsequently do (persistent file/network access without re-prompting). Scope is explicitly limited to skill-introduced vulnerabilities assuming a trusted runtime; attacks on the underlying LLM, side channels, and physical access are excluded.
limitations: Authors are unusually candid: (1) 'Vulnerability' deliberately conflates malicious, negligent, and merely-dangerous patterns—should be read as 'patterns warranting review,' not confirmed malware; of the 87 highest-risk skills, manual review confirmed clear malicious intent in only 23 (26.4%). (2) Analysis is purely static—no skills were executed—so exploitability is inferred, not proven (a pilot dynamic test on 25 high-confidence skills confirmed 72% exploitable; full runtime analysis is future work). (3) Survivorship bias: the 17.3% of skills deleted before collection likely over-represent malware, so prevalence may undercount. (4) The hybrid LLM classifier is non-deterministic (5.5% run-to-run, 9.0% prompt-variant variance), hence the 23-30% range and a recommendation to prioritize static patterns for reproducibility. (5) The 200-skill validation set powers only aggregate metrics; per-category detection accuracy is not statistically validated. (6) Dec 2025 snapshot of an extremely early, unvetted ecosystem—prevalence is explicitly framed as unstable and non-steady-state. (7) False negatives (n=11) come from dynamic URL construction, natural-language obfuscation, and delayed/conditional execution that evade single-pass static analysis.

datasets_used: Authors' own crawl of skills.rest (27,365 pre-filter) and skillsmp.com (15,082 pre-filter) marketplaces = 42,447 skills / 54,980 scripts, filtered to 31,132 unique skills / 3,574 scripts (full analysis dataset, Dec 2025)
500-skill taxonomy-development sample (open coding to derive the 14 patterns)
300-skill rule-calibration sample (regex/threshold tuning; 100-skill subset used for LLM-Guard tuning)
200-skill manually annotated validation/ground-truth set (63 vulnerable, 31.5% base rate; Cohen's kappa 0.83 binary)
1,218-skill stratified categorized sample (3.9% of dataset) across 8 functional categories, used for per-category prevalence
5,326 unique skill authors' repository metadata (account age, followers, repos) for publisher risk profiling
quantitative_results: 26.1% of 31,132 skills flagged with >=1 dangerous pattern (8,126 skills); uncertainty range 23-30%, Rogan-Gladen adjusted 26.5%
Severity split: high 5.2% (1,619), medium 8.1% (2,522), low 12.8% (3,985) - counted once at highest severity
By category: data exfiltration 13.3% (4,133), privilege escalation 11.8% (3,671), supply chain 7.4% (2,296), prompt injection 0.7% (209)
SkillScan detection performance: 86.7% precision, 82.5% recall, F1 84.6% (aggregate, n=200); IPW-adjusted 84.5% / 83.8% / 84.1%
Static-scanner-only stage: 71.4% precision, 91.2% recall (hybrid trades +15.3pp precision for -8.7pp recall)
Per-category detection F1: data exfil 88.9%, prompt injection 83.5%, supply chain 83.1%, privilege escalation 82.9%
Script-bundling skills: 40.6% vuln rate vs 24.2% instruction-only, OR=2.12, 95% CI 1.93-2.33, p<0.001
Skill size >500 lines OR=2.14 (CI 1.62-2.83); >=5 external deps OR=1.58 (p=.002); active maintenance OR=0.91 p=.47 (not significant)
Security/Red-team raw flag rate 67.4% -> adjusted ~21.4% after excluding legitimate-tooling patterns; System Admin 54.5%; Documentation lowest at 19.4%

14 vulnerability patterns across 4 categories; e.g. E2 Env-var harvesting 127 skills, SC1 unpinned deps 156, PE1 excessive perms 94, SC2 external script fetch 67, SC3 obfuscated code 55 (counts from 1,218 sample)
44.2% of vulnerable skills (3,594) span >=2 categories; strongest co-occurrence Supply Chain -> Data Exfiltration P(DE|SC)=81%
Categorized-sample aggregate rate 38.7% (471/1,218), IPW-adjusted to 27.3% (reconciles with 26.1% full-dataset rate)
Pilot dynamic validation on 25 high-confidence skills: 72% exhibited exploitable behavior
baselines_compared: Static-only detection vs hybrid (static + LLM) - the paper's own ablation showing the precision/recall tradeoff
General-purpose scanners Semgrep and Bandit reported as near-zero recall on instruction-level threats (Appendix D), motivating the custom skill-security-scan tool
LLM-Guard v0.3.14 (10 chained scanners) as one detection stage; Claude 3.5 Sonnet (temp 0) as the hybrid classifier

Contextual comparison to prior/cited work (NOT the paper's own measurements): MCP-server study found 7.2% vulnerable / 5.5% tool poisoning [15]; VS Code 5.6% of 52,880 extensions suspicious [10]; early browser extensions ~25% requesting dangerous permissions [7]; Chrome study 4,410 extensions stealing queries [11]
